nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically found nestled among branching corals (Pocillopora, Acropora) in shallow tropical reef environments

diet.notes: Has been reported to accept frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, chopped table shrimp, and frozen carnivore preparations in captivity. Target feeding with a syringe or feeding stick is generally advised in reef systems, as this species tends not to compete well with faster tankmates for food. Multiple small daily feedings are preferable to a single large meal.

summary: The Madagascar Spotted Croucher Goby (Caracanthus madagascariensis) is a small, sedentary scorpionfish from the Western Indian Ocean, rarely exceeding 5 cm. Despite the common name, it is not a true goby. Generally considered peaceful, though its dorsal spines are venomous.
careNotes:
The Madagascar Spotted Croucher Goby belongs to the family Scorpaenidae — the scorpionfishes — despite its goby-like common name; it is not a true goby. The species tends to wedge itself motionlessly among branching corals such as Pocillopora and Acropora and rarely swims in open water, making it best housed in an established reef aquarium with plenty of branching coral structure and live rock rubble. A minimum tank size of around 120 litres is generally recommended.

Venomous dorsal spines: The dorsal-fin spines are venomous, producing reactions comparable to bee stings. Handle with extreme caution and avoid touching the fish directly.

Reef safety: Generally considered reef-safe with caution. Spawning individuals have been observed laying eggs on the underside of coral branches, which can cause localized tissue recession in that area; affected corals have been reported to typically regenerate once the eggs hatch. Monitor closely in SPS-dominant systems.

Conspecific behavior: Specimens have been known to squabble with their own kind in smaller aquariums; keeping the species singly is generally advised, or as a mated pair in larger setups.

Feeding: Target feeding with a syringe or feeding stick once daily is generally advised in reef systems, as this species tends not to compete well with faster tankmates. Once acclimated, most specimens have been reported to accept frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and similar small carnivore preparations.

This species has also been documented with a facultative air-breathing capability, as has its close relative Caracanthus maculatus (the Spotted Coral Croucher Goby). The two are sometimes confused: C. maculatus has a broader Indo-Pacific distribution, while C. madagascariensis is restricted to the Western Indian Ocean from East Africa to the Maldives and Chagos Archipelago.
breedingNotes: Not well-documented in captivity. Based on close congener C. maculatus, egg deposition on SPS coral tissue has been observed in aquaria. Full captive-breeding documentation is absent from available sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5.0 cm TL confirmed), native range (Western Indian Ocean: East Africa to Maldives and Chagos Archipelago — more authoritative than LiveAquaria's single 'Madagascar' origin), and wild temperature habitat (24.8–28.9°C vs LiveAquaria's 72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C). Temperature ranges averaged to 23–27°C. FishBase also confirmed family (Scorpaenidae), order (Perciformes), and facultative air-breathing capability. Reef2Reef wiki returned HTTP 403.

fish.breedingNotes: Not well-documented in captivity; egg deposition on SPS coral tissue has been inferred from congener behavior. Full captive-breeding records are absent.
